title: Lamb vindaloo
subtitle: The hottest standard curry on the menu
excerpt: Lamb shoulder pieces slow-cooked in a fiercely spiced paste of chilli, garlic, vinegar, and warming spices. Vindaloo's defining character is heat from a large quantity of dried chillies, a sharp vinegar note, and a dark, almost sour sauce that absorbs deep into the meat over a long braise. The British curry-house benchmark for maximum heat.
sourceNotes: Vindaloo derives from the Goan dish carne de vinha d'alhos (meat marinated in wine vinegar and garlic), brought to Goa by Portuguese traders and transformed by the addition of Indian spices. The British curry-house version amplifies the chilli heat significantly beyond the Goan original.

content.text: Vindaloo requires marinating the lamb before cooking. The vinegar in the marinade begins to break down the meat fibres, which is part of what gives vindaloo its distinctive texture — slightly more tender and the spice flavours more deeply absorbed than curries without a marinade. One hour is the minimum; overnight is better.

The chilli level here is high. This recipe uses 3 teaspoons of chilli powder plus dried chillies in the paste — that is the appropriate heat for a dish whose purpose is to be the hottest option on the menu. If the result is not conspicuously hot, it is not vindaloo. Adjust downward only if the context requires it.

Combine the {{lamb-shoulder}} with the {{white-wine-vinegar}}, 1 teaspoon of the {{chilli-powder}}, and the {{turmeric}} in a bowl. Toss well to coat. Cover and refrigerate for at least 1 hour, or overnight.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a large heavy pan over high heat. Remove the lamb from the marinade (reserving the marinade) and brown in batches for 3 minutes per batch. Remove and set aside. Reduce heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 12 minutes until golden. Add the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, remaining {{chilli-powder}}, {{cumin-ground}}, and {{coriander-ground}} and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and the reserved marinade and cook for 8 minutes until thickened. Return the lamb to the pan. Add the {{potato}} and 250 ml water. Bring to the boil, cover, and simmer for 60 to 70 minutes until the lamb is tender. Remove the lid for the final 10 minutes to reduce the sauce. Stir in the {{garam-masala}} and season with salt.

items.fix: Add a pinch more chilli powder at the end, stirring in and tasting. Kashmiri chilli powder gives colour but less heat — use a hotter variety for vindaloo

items.cause: Marinade not balanced or too much vinegar
items.fix: Add a pinch of sugar. The vinegar should be in the background, not the dominant flavour

items.cause: Added too early or cooked too long
items.fix: Add the potato halfway through the braise rather than at the start. They should hold their shape

items.symptom: Sauce too dark and thick too soon
items.fix: Reduce to the lowest simmer, add a splash of water, and continue. Scorching ruins the sauce

content.text: Vindaloo occupies a specific cultural position in the British curry-house: it is the maximum heat option, the test of seriousness, and a regular fixture in competitive ordering between groups. This reputation somewhat obscures its origins — the Goan vindalho is a well-balanced, sour-warm-spiced dish, not simply the hottest thing available. The British version amplifies the chilli element to the point where the dish became a category of experience rather than a regional recipe. Both versions are worth knowing.
